title: When does a one-time payment post to the account
summary: Explain how quickly a one-time payment made through chat, app, or web reflects on the customer balance and what can delay posting.
symptoms: customer paid but still sees past-due
payment confirmation received but balance not updated
common_causes: card authorization held for up to 15 minutes before posting
bank transfer (ACH) takes 1-3 business days to clear
payment submitted after daily cutoff at 11:59 PM ET
resolution_steps: Confirm the payment method — card payments post within 15 minutes; bank transfers take 1-3 business days.
Check the email or in-app confirmation for the payment reference number.
If more than 3 business days have passed without posting, capture the reference number and escalate.
Reassure the customer that late fees will be reversed automatically once the payment posts within the expected window.
escalation_policy: Escalate to the payments operations team if an ACH payment has not posted after 3 business days.

title: Enrolling in autopay — eligibility and first-draft timing
summary: What customers should expect when they enroll in autopay, including eligibility, the first-draft cycle, and payment method rules.
symptoms: customer wants to enroll in autopay
asks when the first autopay charge will happen
asks whether autopay gives a discount
common_causes: customer has a past-due balance that must be cleared before enrollment
customer has no saved payment method
enrollment happens too close to the next bill cycle to apply this month
resolution_steps: Confirm the account has no past-due balance; autopay cannot be enabled while past-due is > $0.
Verify at least one saved payment method (card or bank) is on file.
Explain that autopay drafts 5 days before the bill due date; enrollment at least 7 days before the next due date guarantees it applies that cycle.
Mention the $5 autopay credit applies starting the FIRST successfully drafted cycle, not the enrollment cycle.
Use propose_autopay to surface the confirmation button; only the click enrolls.
escalation_policy: Escalate to billing operations if the customer has cleared their past-due balance but the system blocks autopay enrollment.

title: Split-payment across two cards or bank accounts
summary: Rules for splitting a single bill across multiple payment methods in one session.
symptoms: customer wants to pay with two different cards
asks if a partial payment is allowed
common_causes: customer hit per-card daily limit
customer wants to pay some now and the rest after payday
resolution_steps: Confirm that partial (under-balance) one-time payments ARE allowed — any positive amount up to the total balance.
Explain that the chat supports ONE payment per propose_payment; to split, make two separate payments in the same conversation.
Walk through the amount for the first card, propose_payment, confirm, then repeat for the second card.
Remind the customer that a partial payment does not cancel late-fee eligibility — they must clear the full past-due amount to avoid the next fee.
escalation_policy: Escalate to billing if a customer needs a formal payment arrangement for an amount larger than their balance.

title: Payment methods we accept and daily limits
summary: Accepted payment method types and per-method daily limits.
symptoms: customer asks which cards are accepted
common_causes: customer tried a prepaid card with insufficient funds
customer exceeded the $2,500 daily card limit
customer tried a foreign card without USD billing address
resolution_steps: Confirm accepted types: Visa, Mastercard, American Express, Discover, US-issued debit cards, and US bank-account ACH transfers.
Explain the $2,500 daily limit per card and the $10,000 daily limit per bank account.
If the card was declined, ask the customer to verify billing address and CVV, or switch to a bank account for larger amounts.
Do NOT accept prepaid cards, gift cards, or non-US-issued cards.
escalation_policy: Escalate to fraud-ops if a card is repeatedly declined and the customer asserts the bank approved the charge.

title: Refund windows and timing for overpayments
summary: When and how an overpayment is refunded back to the original payment method.
symptoms: customer paid more than their balance and wants the difference back
common_causes: duplicate payment submitted within the same day
autopay draft ran the same day as a manual payment
resolution_steps: Confirm the overpayment amount by comparing billing_list_charges to the current balance.
Explain default behavior: overpayments are applied as an account credit toward the NEXT bill.
If the customer wants the money returned to the card instead, note it takes 5-7 business days to post back to the original payment method.
For amounts under $25, do NOT issue a refund to the card — the bank processing fee usually exceeds the refund; keep as account credit.
Escalate to billing-ops to initiate the return-to-card refund when over $25.
escalation_policy: Escalate to billing operations for any refund-to-card request over $25.

title: Late fees and fee-waiver eligibility
summary: When late fees apply and the policy for waiving a late fee as a courtesy.
symptoms: customer asks to waive a fee
common_causes: bill went unpaid past the due date
autopay drafted but the bank returned insufficient funds
resolution_steps: Confirm the fee amount: $10 standard late fee applied 5 days after the due date.
Check billing_list_charges for the customer's payment history over the last 12 months.
Waiver eligibility: the account must have no more than ONE waived late fee in the prior 12 months and must be otherwise in good standing.
If eligible, confirm the waiver in text; do NOT claim the waiver is applied. Waivers require billing-ops approval — escalate using the escalation contact.
Encourage autopay enrollment to avoid future late fees (propose_autopay if they have a payment method on file).
escalation_policy: Fee waivers always route through billing operations; the agent cannot apply waivers directly.

title: Setting a new default payment method
summary: How the default payment method is used and what changing it does to autopay.
symptoms: customer added a new card and wants autopay to use it
customer asks which card is being charged
common_causes: customer added a new card without updating autopay
customer cancelled the old card
resolution_steps: Call payment_method_list to identify the current default (is_default: true).
Confirm with the customer which method should be the new default.
Use propose_payment_method_set_default with the target payment_method_id — the click-through updates both the default flag and the autopay source if enrolled.
Explain that the change takes effect for the NEXT autopay draft; any draft already scheduled in the next 24 hours uses the previous method.
escalation_policy: Escalate to billing-ops if a default change fails repeatedly.

title: Autopay draft failed — insufficient funds or declined card
summary: What happens when the autopay draft fails and how the account recovers.
symptoms: customer got a text that their autopay failed
account is now past-due after autopay date
common_causes: insufficient funds in the bank account
card expired
bank flagged the charge as fraud
resolution_steps: Look up the current balance via billing_get_balance to confirm the failure.
Ask the customer to verify the payment method on file (payment_method_list) and update if the card is expired.
Offer to make a one-time payment now (propose_payment) to clear the past-due balance.
Explain that a single failed autopay does NOT un-enroll the customer — autopay will retry on the next cycle using the same method unless the customer switches it.
Three consecutive failures auto-suspend autopay; in that case recommend re-enrolling with a different method after the current balance is cleared.
escalation_policy: Escalate to billing operations if the customer's autopay has been auto-suspended after 3 failures.

title: How autopay credit and paperless-billing credit combine
summary: Which customer-facing credits stack and how they appear on the bill.
symptoms: customer asks why they aren't getting the $10 credit they expected
customer wants to know if discounts stack
common_causes: customer enrolled in autopay but not paperless billing
customer is using a bank account (qualifies for full credit) vs. a card (partial)
resolution_steps: Confirm the rules: autopay credit is $5/month when paid by bank account, $3/month when paid by card. Paperless-billing credit is $5/month.
Both credits can stack — maximum combined credit is $10/month when using bank + paperless.
Credits appear as a line item on the bill AFTER the first successful autopay draft and the first paperless-delivered statement.
If a customer expected a credit but doesn't see it, confirm both enrollments are active and that at least one successful autopay cycle has completed.
escalation_policy: Escalate to billing-ops if both enrollments are active for more than one full cycle but the credit has not posted.
